request.question: NFL Street 3 is the third installment of the "NFL Street" series, which  American football wide receiver, was an official spokesperson of the game?

title: NFL Street 2
Braylon Edwards
Johnnie Morant
NFL Tour
Bumpin' My Music
NFL Street 3
J. D. Hill
Chad Johnson
NFL Street
Kenny Stafford
sentences: NFL Street 2 is American football video game developed by EA Sports BIG and published by Electronic Arts.
It was originally released for the PlayStation 2, GameCube and Xbox on December 22, 2004.
It features then-New York Giants tight end Jeremy Shockey and rapper Xzibit on the cover.
The game is compatible with "Madden NFL 2006", as players can import their "Own The City" players to it.
The game's compatibility also extends to other EA games like "", "SSX 3", "Madden NFL 2005", "NCAA Football 2005", "NFL Street", "", "NBA Live 2005", and "NBA Street Vol.
2", where if players have these games on their memory cards, they can earn 25,000 points in the game.
Braylon Jamel Edwards (born February 21, 1983) is a former American football wide receiver in the National Football League (NFL).
He played college football for the University of Michigan, earned unanimous All-American honors, and was recognized as the top college wide receiver.
He was also the first receiver in Big Ten Conference history to record three consecutive 1,000-yard seasons and only the third to do so in NCAA Division I-A.
He was selected by the Cleveland Browns with the third overall pick in the 2005 NFL Draft.
Johnnie Morant, Jr. (born December 7, 1981) is a former gridiron football wide receiver.
He was drafted by the Oakland Raiders in the fifth round of the 2004 NFL Draft.
He played college football at Syracuse.
He is currently the Head Strength and Conditioning Coach and Wide Receivers coach at East Carter High School.
While at Kentucky Christian University, Coach Morant help mentor an All-American Wide Receiver who was invited to an All Star game and an All Mid-South Academic Wide Receiver.
NFL Tour is an arcade football video game.
It was released on January 8, 2008.
It was developed under the EA Sports BIG division of EA Sports, which is also responsible for such titles as "SSX", "NBA Street", "NFL Street", and the "FIFA Street" games.
The game could be considered a successor to the "NFL Street" franchise, but it was converted to the new hardware (PlayStation 3 / Xbox 360).
Trey Wingo from ESPN is the game's voice announcer.
"Bumpin' My Music" is a 2006 single by Ray Cash as his third single from "C.O.D. (Cash on Delivery)".
This song is Cash's first single to reach any chart in "Billboard" magazine or to be featured on a countdown.
/ /chart .
The track is the third song on the album with a running time of 4:01.
Scarface, who is an idol of Ray Cash is featured on the song.
For a period of time, it was featured on BET 106 & Park voting page.
It was also featured on the video game NFL Street 3.
NFL Street 3 is the third installment of the "NFL Street" series, released in November 2006 for the PlayStation 2 and PlayStation Portable consoles.
This installment featured more game modes and unlockable features than previous versions.
Chad Johnson of the Cincinnati Bengals appears on the cover and was the official spokesperson of the game.

J. D. Hill (born October 30, 1948, in Stockton, California) is a retired American football wide receiver who played seven seasons in the National Football League for the Buffalo Bills and Detroit Lions; he played college football at Arizona State University.
His son, Lonzell Hill, also played wide receiver in the NFL.
Another son, Shelby Hill, was a wide receiver for Syracuse University.
Chad Javon Johnson (born January 9, 1978), formerly Chad Ochocinco, is a former American football wide receiver.

He played college football for both Santa Monica College and Oregon State University, and played eleven seasons in the National Football League (NFL) for the Cincinnati Bengals, New England Patriots, and the Miami Dolphins.
He was drafted by the Cincinnati Bengals of the National Football League (NFL) in the second round of the 2001 NFL Draft, and played for the Bengals for 10 seasons.
In 2011, Johnson was traded to the New England Patriots which he played for in Super Bowl XLVI.
In 2012, Johnson played for the Miami Dolphins during preseason but was released following his arrest for domestic violence.

He played for the Montreal Alouettes of the Canadian Football League (CFL) from 2014 to 2015, and played one game in 2017 for Mexican team Monterrey Fundidores of the Liga de Fútbol Americano Profesional.
NFL Street is an American football video game developed by EA Tiburon and published by Electronic Arts.
It was originally released for the PlayStation 2, GameCube and Xbox on January 13, 2004.
Barry Sanders of the Detroit Lions, Shannon Sharpe of the Denver Broncos, and Ricky Williams of the Miami Dolphins grace the cover.
The game was followed by "NFL Street 2" and "NFL Street 3".
Kenny Stafford (born April 20, 1990) is a Canadian football wide receiver who is currently a member of the Edmonton Eskimos of the Canadian Football League (CFL).
In the United States he has been a member of the Atlanta Falcons (NFL), Pittsburgh Power (AFL) and Miami Dolphins (NFL).
Elsewhere in the CFL Stafford has been a member of the Calgary Stampeders, Montreal Alouettes and Winnipeg Blue Bombers.
He is the nephew of former American football wide receiver and 2013 Hall of Fame Inductee Cris Carter.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.hop1_query: NFL Street 3 is the third installment of the "NFL Street" series, which  American football wide receiver, was an official spokesperson of the game?
hop1_docs: NFL Street 3 | NFL Street 3 is the third installment of the "NFL Street" series, released in November 2006 for the PlayStation 2 and PlayStation Portable consoles.  This installment featured more game modes and unlockable features than previous versions.  Chad Johnson of the Cincinnati Bengals appears on the cover and was the official spokesperson of the game.

Tai Streets | Tai Lamar Streets (born April 20, 1977) is a former professional American football wide receiver in the National Football League (NFL).  He was selected with the second pick of the sixth round of the 1999 NFL Draft by the San Francisco 49ers.  He also played for the Detroit Lions in 2004.  He was the leading receiver for the national champion 1997 Michigan Wolverines football team.  Over the course of his career he was notable for fourth quarter performances in various bowl games and NFL playoff games.  As a professional athlete, he was known for his modesty.  As an amateur athlete, he was known as one of the best high school athletes in the city of Chicago.

NFL Street (series) | NFL Street is a series of sports video games developed by EA Tiburon and published by Electronic Arts.  It combines the talent and big names of the NFL with the atmosphere of street football.  It is much like the regular Madden NFL series, however plays are, in fact different.

Devin Street | Devin Curtis Street (born March 30, 1991) is an American football wide receiver who is currently a free agent.  He is in the National Football League (NFL).  He was drafted by the Dallas Cowboys in the fifth round of the 2014 NFL Draft.  He played college football at the University of Pittsburgh.

ESPN NFL 2K5 | ESPN NFL 2K5 is an American football video game developed by Visual Concepts and published by 2K Sports and the Sega Corporation.  It is the sixth installment of the "NFL 2K series".  The game was originally released on July 20, 2004, for both the PlayStation 2 and Xbox video game consoles.  It was the last "NFL 2K" game to be released before Electronic Arts signed an exclusivity deal with the NFL to make 2K's rival Madden NFL series the only officially licensed NFL game.  It was also the last game to be made by Sega before that happened.

NFL Tour | NFL Tour is an arcade football video game.  It was released on January 8, 2008.  It was developed under the EA Sports BIG division of EA Sports, which is also responsible for such titles as "SSX", "NBA Street", "NFL Street", and the "FIFA Street" games.  The game could be considered a successor to the "NFL Street" franchise, but it was converted to the new hardware (PlayStation 3 / Xbox 360).  Trey Wingo from ESPN is the game's voice announcer.

NFL Street | NFL Street is an American football video game developed by EA Tiburon and published by Electronic Arts.  It was originally released for the PlayStation 2, GameCube and Xbox on January 13, 2004.  Barry Sanders of the Detroit Lions, Shannon Sharpe of the Denver Broncos, and Ricky Williams of the Miami Dolphins grace the cover.  The game was followed by "NFL Street 2" and "NFL Street 3".
response.summary_1:
Header: NFL Street 3 spokesperson and other wide receivers mentioned

- From NFL Street 3 page: "Chad Johnson of the Cincinnati Bengals appears on the cover" and "was the official spokesperson of the game." (passage [1])
- Other wide receivers mentioned in passages: "Tai Lamar Streets" — "former professional American football wide receiver" (born April 20, 1977). (passage [2]); "Devin Curtis Street" — "American football wide receiver" (born March 30, 1991). (passage [4])
- Missing / ambiguous fact: passages do NOT state Chad Johnson's playing position (i.e., whether he is a "wide receiver"); need to verify the bridge entity "Chad Johnson" → position "wide receiver" and possible name variant ("Chad Ochocinco").
- Next-hop retrieval (priority order):
  1. Fetch "Chad Johnson (American football)" or "Chad Ochocinco" page — inspect lede/infobox for position (wide receiver) and references to NFL Street 3.
  2. Re-open "NFL Street 3" article — inspect lede/infobox and "Cover" or "Reception" sections to confirm spokesperson credit.
  3. Search keywords if needed: "Chad Johnson NFL Street 3 spokesperson", "Chad Ochocinco NFL Street 3 cover".
  4. If name variants cause ambiguity, fetch disambiguation "Chad Johnson" page.
hop2_docs: Chad Johnson (disambiguation) | Chad Johnson (born 1978; formerly Chad Ochocinco) is an American football wide receiver.

Chad Mock | Chad Mock (born March 23, 1984) is a professional football player who played for the BC Lions of the Canadian Football League in 2007.  He is currently a member of their practice squad.  Chad played the wide receiver position with the Lions, and wore jersey number #83.

Chad Johnson | Chad Javon Johnson (born January 9, 1978), formerly Chad Ochocinco, is a former American football wide receiver.  He played college football for both Santa Monica College and Oregon State University, and played eleven seasons in the National Football League (NFL) for the Cincinnati Bengals, New England Patriots, and the Miami Dolphins.  He was drafted by the Cincinnati Bengals of the National Football League (NFL) in the second round of the 2001 NFL Draft, and played for the Bengals for 10 seasons.  In 2011, Johnson was traded to the New England Patriots which he played for in Super Bowl XLVI.  In 2012, Johnson played for the Miami Dolphins during preseason but was released following his arrest for domestic violence.  He played for the Montreal Alouettes of the Canadian Football League (CFL) from 2014 to 2015, and played one game in 2017 for Mexican team Monterrey Fundidores of the Liga de Fútbol Americano Profesional.

Cobi Hamilton | Cobi Hamilton (born November 13, 1990) is an American football wide receiver for the Houston Texans of the National Football League (NFL).  He played college football at Arkansas.  He was considered one of the top wide receiver prospects for the 2013 NFL Draft.  He was selected by the Cincinnati Bengals with the 197th overall pick in the draft in the sixth round with a selection acquired from the New England Patriots in 2011 for Chad Johnson.

Curtis Johnson (American football coach) | Curtis Johnson, Jr. (born November 5, 1961) is an American football coach who was most recently the wide receivers coach for the New Orleans Saints of the National Football League (NFL).  He was the head coach at Tulane University from 2012 to 2015, a position he assumed in December 2011 after six seasons coaching wide receivers for the New Orleans Saints.

Chad Williams (wide receiver) | Chad Williams (born October 19, 1994) is an American football wide receiver for the Arizona Cardinals of the National Football League (NFL).  He played college football at Grambling State.

Tim Lappano | Tim Lappano is an American football coach, currently the wide receivers coach at Georgia State University in Atlanta.  He has coached a variety of positions at both the collegiate and professional levels, most recently coaching Brandon Pettigrew as the tight ends coach and Calvin Johnson as the wide receivers coach of the Detroit Lions through the 2013 season.
response.summary_2: {"summary":"Chad Johnson of the Cincinnati Bengals appears on the cover and was the official spokesperson of the game. Chad Johnson is a former American football wide receiver who played for the Cincinnati Bengals."}
